speaker: interviewer
text: --Australia, and this is tape four. OK, Peter, so you've just been in hospital with your knee.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the hospital. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're 13-- still 13, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. 13 when I was in the camp. I was already 15--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 15 now. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- 15? Yes. Then, as luck would have it, this, this-- I heard that there are Russian officers in hospital, too. Well, I spoke Russian. And I thought, why should I-- you know? The food wasn't that good, but at least it was food in my stomach. And I went down, and saw the Russian officer. There was a very big Russian general that was in hospital. And I told him my story.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, what are you doing here? And I said, well, now I heard that became USSR where I am, where [NON-ENGLISH] was. Said I-- told him what happened. He said, straightaway, right, you are treated the same as a Russian. And different food and a bed that I-- only two, because you couldn't have more than-- you know, there was no room. And I started to eat good food and walk in the garden a bit, you know? Till I felt a bit better. I mean, still with my foot, I dragged the foot, but it was getting a bit better. I was stuck there. And I spent a few, few weeks there, in hospital, till one day he said to me, he said, now that you're getting a bit better, he said, I'm leaving, too, to Russia. He said, how about you come with us? You might even join up the army to, you know-- although the war was over, but, you know-- his group, or whatever. And I said, that's all I need now. So one morning, when it was still dark, I got up. I left the hospital and went to the train-- again, no money, on top-- and went to Budapest, to see if anybody of the family survived. Because there is transport coming to different places. And the big towns like that, they had transport of people coming. And also it was more or less a meeting place, and there was food provided because the Joint helped to alert the people that were liberated in these areas. So I went there. But unfortunately, I couldn't find anybody. So I decided to go back home to, to my cousin, to be with my cousin, to Bilky and to Irshava with my-- Irshava to my other cousin, to be together with them. And I came home to Loza again-- to, to the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my neighbors, you know, I remember my mother said she left-- she left certain things there with you. And I'd like something back. They said yes, but we haven't got this, and we haven't got it. See, I thought that-- I was looking for the silver, the cutlery, because I don't want anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something to sell.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I'm not going to take any linen. Well, they said they didn't have that. But linen, they've still got linen-- whatever. I said-- it's OK. And I said goodbye to them. Then I went to my neighbors next to us, they had two daughters-- lovely girls. And I used to go to school with them still. So they said, stay the night. You know, sleep the night at our place. And you've still got your property. It can be built, and you've got-- all the fields are there, and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to them, this is the last time that I'll ever step foot in this place. I'm leaving, and I'll got to the other end of the world. I mean, [LAUGHS], not even knowing! I said, and I never want to come back here again. I don't want to see the place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes-- I did go to the school. I went to the school. Oh, I was determined. I went to the school, and I spoke to her, to the teacher. And I spoke to the husband. And I just told them, I just wanted to show you, I have survived. I've come back. And I'll never see this place again.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very nice. He said, look, I'm sorry what happened and that. She just didn't know what to say. She couldn't-- you know? And I left the place. And so it was. I've never, ever stepped back-- Gone back.

speaker: interviewee
text: --gone back. And I don't want to go back. Never seen the place again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed with my cousin, and I said to him, we've got to get out of here. It's no use. I didn't want to be-- didn't want to live there. Because, first of all, to remind me all the time of my family. And I just wanted to get out. And I was thinking of going to Israel-- Palestine, at that time. And I told him that. So what did we have to do? We had to go back to Germany-- to the American section. And from there we would go. Or to Prague, to Czechoslovakia, to see how we can get out. So we decided, my cousin and I-- we decided to get out of the place. And he was already back into his butchery and that, but he agreed that it's not a proposition to live-- not to stay there. So we went to a place called Uzhhorod, which was in the Carpathia, not far from us. And this is an interesting story, too. Uzhhorod, where you got papers to go to, became USSR-- so to go to Czechoslovakia. And I said, we have family in Czechoslovakia. We were born in Czechoslovakia, although the place now is USSR or whatever. But we want to go back to our family. So we went into this office, my cousin and myself, and his wife-- the three of us. It was Efraim. Blanca was her name. She's still alive. She is in New York. And myself. And the guy was very-- with his Russian uniform, you know-- very sort of anti. Why you were running away? Why you are going away? This is-- this is a wonderful country, our country. You shouldn't go away from here. So, the voice. I recognized something in the voice. It's-- and you have to have money. It was on kilos, you know? For to get a stamp or something, you have to give them money that-- you know, it was just unbelievable. The Russian money just-- a kilo of money for something. So we were talking. He was arguing with us and arguing with us. And I said to him, somehow, I know you. I don't know where from, but I know you. I knew the voice and the face. And he said, how would you know me? I've never seen you before. And then I said, have you been to a camp at all? He stopped. I said, have you been to Gusen, Gunskirchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he just put his-- he said, Gunskirchen, yes. I said, I remember you cutting up the bread for us. I remember you telling me the story about what kind of a chef-- cook you were!

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he couldn't believe it. It was him. So he joined the Russian army afterwards. It was him! He said, yes, it is me. Stamped all the stamps. Go! So, I mean-- life, you know? So we went to Prague. From there we went to, to Liberec, which is called Reichenberg-- that's in the Sudeten-- and we stayed a while there. And then I said to him, I am leaving. I'm going back to Germany, and I want to go to Israel. So I-- and you had to also go across the border, back to Germany. And I must say the, the Zionist groups, they had connections with the Israeli-- the Israeli guys were there. And they knew how to get across to the border. So we went across at night back into Germany from, from Liberec.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I omitted something else in Germany. You see, I'm jumping a bit. From Landsberg-- I stayed in a DP camp-- Landsberg am Lech. I don't know if I mentioned it. I don't think I mentioned it. I was in a DP camp in Landsberg am Lech after. But I'll come back to that. No, this is part of the story. It's not-- I haven't omitted it. Then I went to Germany, and that's how I, I went to Landsberg am Lech. It was a big DP camp. And I stayed there for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany. This is after the war now, we're talking about. Landsberg am Lech, that's where Hitler wrote And we were looked after with the Joint. The Joint Distribution Committee was looking after us. And they-- I mean, the food was provided, the accommodation was provided. This is already when I came from Czechoslovakia back into Germany. And there wanted to go to, to Israel-- Palestine, at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to a kibbutz in Holzhausen, which is next door to Landsberg am Lech. And from there, we were supposed to go to, to Israel. And that was a very, very nice place. The American gave it to us-- the Americans-- mostly the youngsters.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was in a convent. There was a convent there. So the nuns looked after us because it was a nice place, and we really started to get our energy back. Because we were still, you know, very, very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Debilitated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I stayed in Holzhausen for a while. And then there was a transport going to Palestine on the ship And I was going to be on that transport, too. And then they said, look, you're still young. There are older people that should be-- should go, and you haven't been on the-- on the wait-- you didn't wait as long as they have been waiting. We better send them first.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was not too happy about that. I didn't want to stay in Germany anymore. So I got out of the kibbutz, and that's when I went back to Landsberg. And then, from Landsberg am Lech, they, they send us to Prien am Chiemsee, which is a very, very beautiful spot in Germany, to-- again-- to-- Recuperate.

speaker: interviewee
text: --recuperate. That was a beautiful place. You know, we're near the water, and we-- and they also gave us good food. And we started to really feel on top of the world then as far as the health is concerned. And I didn't want to stay in Germany, so I went to Munich, to the committee, and I said, I've got an uncle in America. I remember that. So I said, I want to go to America if I can't go to Palestine. I don't want to stay here anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how the paper started to, to be processed. But I didn't have a birth certificate, and the Americans wanted to have a birth certificate. So eventually, my cousin was still in Czechoslovakia-- sent me some papers. Either a birth certificate he got-- I can't remember what papers. In the meantime, somebody said on the committee there in Munich said, do you want to go to Australia? Because if you want to go to Australia, it's no problem. You don't have to wait too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said OK, let it be Australia. But I want to get out of here. And that's how I, I went from Germany to France and from France to Australia. This is more or less what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never heard of Australia! I mean, I'd heard of Australia, but  I didn't know what was going on here. But I just came to a stage where I just couldn't face Germany. I just had to get out. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: I suppose you thought you could always leave, eventually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I thought if things don't work out in Australia, I can always go to America, because the papers were still, you know, being processed, and I'll write to America to-- not to America-- to Munich to send me on the papers. But I decided to come here. I mean, it's all just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Fate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fate! Everything is fate. It is. And luck. I mean, that I survived, where I've been, is just-- well, it's luck. It's faith. It's-- you name it. It's, it's something that happened. Not because of anything, it's just--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many didn't survive? Six million didn't survive. And I survived. So it's-- So you think-- I mean, do you think there's any reason why you survived and those other people didn't? Or was it just luck and fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. It's hard to tell. But I, I tried to survive, number one. I tried. I didn't give in. I never gave in for one minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I've also-- and also lucky that I wasn't shot, as I said, the person next to me. So that is luck. That I have this girl in Auschwitz coming to me and telling me to say I'm older. Why would she say something like that? I mean-- So, what is it? Is it-- it's a miracle, anyway. I mean, it's-- I don't know the answer. I don't know the answer. But I, I survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister survived. I didn't know-- years later, when I was in Australia, I was looking-- looking. Because I heard somebody said that she survived. And she was liberated by the Russians. So she went to Russia first. And that's how I couldn't-- I couldn't get in touch with her till later. Years later, I found out that she is alive in Russia-- married. And, of course, I've got a picture of her, too, which I'll show you after. And she's living in Israel today with two children that she's got-- a son and a daughter-- and gorgeous grandchildren, which we are very close with together. We keep in touch, and we visit each other. And they're just beautiful people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there you are. From a family of six children and aunts and uncles, two of us survived from that family. And she had a terrible, terrible life, too, in the camps, that she told me. So this is-- this is what happened. So tell me about coming to Australia. You came to Melbourne, originally, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, what happened was we came from-- the Jewish Welfare Society sent us the papers. And we came to Melbourne from-- first of all, we went through France on an Egyptian ship, And the war was on in Israel at that time, in 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was a very interesting trip, here. We had to watch ourselves, because they had an Egyptian crew! Oh, gosh.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] And everything on top of it. And we arrived to Melbourne. And to Melbourne, we were straight away transferred to Sydney. But we were originally meant to go to Melbourne. I don't know the reason for it. But it was all done through the Jewish Welfare Society. And they send us on to Sydney by train. And they met us on the railway station. I remember Mr. Brandt, who was there, met us. And we were taken to the Isabella Lazarus Home, in Hunters Hill. And we stayed there for a little while, and learned-- learning English-- a few words in English, because we couldn't speak English, so they had to teach it-- taught us English. And afterwards we sort of had to find our own way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you, in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was here, I must have been 16, 16 only, or 16 and 1/2. Still very young. And we had to find ourselves jobs, as well. So that's another story about life here. It was not easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they help you to find jobs at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: They tried to help us, yes. But the thing that was very good-- and I was very lucky here, again-- is Rabbi Porush and Mrs. Porush-- I don't know if you remember them. And they tried to find for the boys-- were a group of young boys-- they tried to find somewhere for Friday night, for dinner, you know? For Shabbat. And I was fortunate to find very nice people-- or they found me, or they send me to them-- called the Van Rouens. They were just absolutely wonderful. I came for a Friday night. First of all, I was reluctant to go because I remember at home, in the synagogue, when people didn't have where to go, they used to line up at the door, and everybody took somebody home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: For a Friday night. But it wasn't like that at all. Went there, and Joyce picked me up. And we're still the best like this today. She couldn't speak to me, and I couldn't speak to her. But her husband was Dutch. Van Rouen, he was Dutch, and he spoke German. So I spoke German. And I met her, her brothers, which helped a lot because we became very close friends. And then she said-- they both said-- well, come again the next Friday night. And I went the next Friday night and the other Friday night. And they also brought their niece-- or his niece-- that was saved by the Dutch people. She was in Holland, little Grita. She is-- she is, to me, like a sister today. And so they had her. And I was there another-- you know, a few Friday nights.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, one day, there was a key on the table, on a Friday night. And whenever you are lonely, you're around here in the eastern suburbs, please just open the door like your own home, you know? So, of course, that made all the difference to my life here. So tell us about meeting your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. That was lovely. She was singing. She was singing at a concert at the Maccabean Hall. You know, and we went to the Maccabean Hall because that was a meeting place, to meet other people-- Jewish people, too. And I saw her singing onstage. And I thought, gee, what a-- I'm going-- that's years, years later. What a beautiful girl! I'd love to meet her. And, you know, being me, I-- [LAUGHS] I don't know, I just-- I didn't speak that time. Or I did, but I-- I liked her, and I asked who she was. And people told me that she entertains. She sings at Jewish functions, and also she was singing for sheepskins for Russia, once, in the town hall in those days. And, through a mutual friend, we met. And this is-- we fell in love. Or I fell in love with her. Took her a little bit longer for me to persuade her, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to be deterred, you went out again?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. And, uh--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you marry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, dear. We're married 40 years, now, so it's '50--

speaker: interviewer
text: '55 or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what did you do for a living, in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started to-- I learned cutting and designing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fabric.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fabric, yes. First of all, I went to a weaving mill-- that was my first job-- to learn about the fabrics. And then I went to technical college to learn cutting and designing. And I got a job as a cutter. And from then on I've been in the clothing trade ever since.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, now you're the boss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now I own my own business. Yes, I don't know if I'm the boss, but-- [LAUGHS] I've got my own--

speaker: interviewer
text: Apart from Rosie.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me how many children you have now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got three children-- two boys and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you just had your first grandchild.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just had, this week, my first grandchild, which I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to see a photo of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'm very happy, very proud. So there you are, out of-- out of [SOBBING] where I was, to come to this. My greatest joy. So God was good to me. If you believe in God, well, there you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still believe in God?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I do. I do. I certainly do. And that kept me going, too, because we believed-- never gave up. But I do ask questions. Where was he--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just one last--

speaker: interviewee
text: --at a terrible time?

speaker: interviewer
text: One last thing, Peter, is, on this tape, which will go to your family, what message would you like to give your family in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, message, first of all, I love them very dearly. They are all so precious to me. And you can imagine, the beginning, I came here by myself, after losing everybody. [SOBBING] I met the most beautiful woman. She's absolutely-- I'm very, very lucky. I'm blessed with the most wonderful wife any human being can have. And I mean that. And then to have the children, which are gorgeous children-- beautiful children-- in every-- in every way. I absolutely adore them, and I am blessed to have them. And I'm very grateful to God that he gave me that, to have a family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I bless them, and I wish them all the luck in the world. And they should just keep being the way they are. They're good human beings. And what else can I say? That just I'm very proud of them, very, very proud of them. And soon you'll see them. They're going to come in. They want to say something, too. So I've been blessed. With all the troubles that I had in life, this makes up for it, because I've got a wonderful, wonderful life with my family. And that's all that counts.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm trying, also, to put in a bit to, to other things, here, in the community, seeing the community's been very good to us, too. You know, especially the Jewish community have been marvelous. When I arrived, I would've been really lost without them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you put your bit back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm trying to put a little bit back, to-- and-- and-- doing my best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences may have changed you as a person from what you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when you say changed me, what the Germans did to us, I can't forget. I can't forget. And I still can't, for the life of me, understand how it could have happened. I cannot understand, and I'm-- I don't know. I just don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet, I can't blame all the Germans. And the young generation-- how can I say that, that they-- that it's their fault, too? I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's-- I've never been back to Germany since then, since I left it. I do want to. Before I die, I do want to go back one day. And my sister wants to go back to Auschwitz to say Kaddish for my parents because-- and my brothers and sisters [SOBBING] because that's where they-- that's where they died. But other than that, I have no interest to go back to Germany or to the other places. Just have no--

speaker: interviewer
text: Desire to go back.

speaker: interviewee
text: --no desire. No, none whatsoever. So this is the story what happened. But as i say, I count my lucky stars, and I thank God every day for my family and for my wonderful wife, really. And I only hope that I'm worthy of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to meet her, soon. So thank you for telling us your story, Peter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry if I broke down, but I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Understandable.

speaker: interviewee
text: --couldn't help it. I don't want them to see me in that--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, that's OK. You've got some time. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Peter, can you tell us about this photograph-- who the people are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my grandparents, from my mother's side. And one of them-- I think one of them is my mother. I don't know. I can't tell you because that's the only picture I have. And that was from my cousin in America. He sent me that. Because that's his grandparents, too. That's my mother's side.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the girls is your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been the bigger one, because she was-- I think she was the eldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Mukacheve.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where she was born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year do you think that-- approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was before she got married, so it would have been-- oh-- it looks to me as though it could be 60, 70 years--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: --ago. Or it would be more. Would be around about 65, 70 years ago, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. OK, Peter, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father. This one, here, is my father. And that's the group of boys that were in the army together-- the Czech army.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the Czech army. And when was that, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was also before. I would say that would've been taken when I was a little boy. Would have been about 50, 60, 60, 65 years ago-- roughly 65 years, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- no, he was with this group in the Czech army. Where it was, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know-- OK. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm glad I've got that. That's the only thing I've got from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: The only photo of your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only photo I've got of my father, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I'm glad I got that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he would have been much older when you remember him, would you? Oh, that was when you were a little boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I was a little boy, yes. So that's why, when I mentioned about what happened in Auschwitz, that they would've-- there were a lot of the boys together from the army, and they would've been the ones--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who blew up the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --that blew up the crematorium. That's what I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us when these photographs were taken, Peter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was taken in 1945, near Landsberg am Lech and Holzhausen. That's where I went to the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were some of the survivors of the-- this one, here, is my cousin. He survived, too. He is in America now. And that was just in the kibbutz, in Holzhausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Isn't that a picture of you on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this one is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a picture of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. That one is me, too, on the left, there. Same picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you'd started eating again. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: After I'd been eating and staying in that kibbutz. As you can see, I've put on a lot of weight. But you can see how young I was still, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that? 1940--?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Just quickly, who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're just friends-- also survivors. We went right through to-- we lived in the DP camp together, and then we went to the kibbutz together. They're just friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you in that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm here-- right here.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's second from the right-- yes, OK. Thank you. OK, Peter, will you tell us what this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is a-- this is a certificate-- not a certificate, but it's a piece of paper that my son went to New York-- about restitution. You know, I put in an application. I hadn't received any restitution yet, so I put in an application. And they are asking for certain papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my son Michael travels quite often to New York, so I asked him to go in there and take some of my papers that I sent to them. And apparently they pulled out a file, and there was my name with all my-- the camps that I'd been in, as you can see. But they've got the-- The records.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the records there. So that's one of those which I thought I'll show you. That gives you an idea of the camps. And they give you the dates and everything, there. I didn't even know they had that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's--
